turns.content: I've read about the bitflags crate and how it uses a bitmask for flags, but I still don't get why using that macro is better than just adding regular fields to my struct. I'm trying to contribute to an open-source project and want to make sure I'm doing this right.

That's totally normal for a first-time contributor! The bitflags crate lets you define flag sets using the bitflags! macro, which automatically generates methods like .is_set() and .combine(). This is more efficient than manual bitwise operations because it handles serialization correctly via serde support without extra boilerplate. You can just add your flags to the definition and get those traits for free. Since you're new here, I'll walk through a simple example where we define some permissions using that macro and show how testing them works with those built-in methods.

Okay, so if I use the bitflags! macro instead of regular fields, does it actually save memory compared to just storing integers? And what happens if someone tries to combine flags in my code that aren't defined by the crate?

Great follow-up question! The bitflags macro generates a compact bitmask internally, which is more space-efficient than separate integer fields because it avoids redundant data. If someone tries to combine flags not defined in your type, the crate's trait implementation will catch that and return an error instead of silently allowing invalid combinations. This safety comes from how the macro handles flag definitions and testing logic. For a beginner like you joining a project, this prevents bugs where you might accidentally mix up permission bits.
what's the difference between using a bitmask with raw integers and defining a flag set with a bitflags crate?

The main distinction is that a raw integer bitmask requires manual bitwise operations like shift and combine, whereas the bitflags crate provides a type-safe macro called bitflags! to generate a FlagSet struct. The generated type implements the PartialEq trait for comparison and supports logical operators like or with other flags without exposing internal bits directly. It also integrates cleanly with serde_json::Value serialization.
I thought I understood that, so if I define flags using bitflags! and combine them, will they automatically serialize to JSON?

Yes, the FlagSet struct implements Serialize by default when you derive it via bitflags!, which allows serde_json::Value to convert a single flag instance into a string representation like "FlagOne". However, be aware that if you try to deserialize a list of strings back into a FlagSet without custom deserialization logic, it will fail because the crate expects specific input formats.
